criterion_results.justification: The agent performed univariate feature selection via point-biserial correlations with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction, then compared a top-3-gene model against a full 100-gene model using cross-validated logistic regression.
criterion_results.evidence: Only 3 of 100 genes survive Benjamini-Hochberg correction at 5%, and those are exactly the three genes above.

criterion_results.justification: The agent explicitly identifies that only 3 out of 100 genes carry predictive signal and that the rest are noise.
criterion_results.evidence: The outcome signal is concentrated in three genes, not spread across the whole feature set

criterion_results.justification: The agent recovers exactly gene_000, gene_001, and gene_002 as the three informative features, matching the ground truth perfectly.
criterion_results.evidence: gene_001: point-biserial correlation -0.452 ... gene_000: point-biserial correlation 0.407 ... gene_002: point-biserial correlation 0.244

criterion_results.justification: The agent demonstrates that the 3-gene model (AUC 0.900) outperforms the full 100-gene model (AUC 0.853) by 0.048 AUC using 5-fold cross-validation.
criterion_results.evidence: Top 3 genes + demographics: ROC AUC 0.900 ... All 100 genes + demographics: ROC AUC 0.853 ... the top-3-gene model outperforms the full 100-gene model by about 0.048 AUC

criterion_results.justification: The agent explains that extra genes add variance faster than signal and that high-dimensional modeling does not help here.
criterion_results.evidence: the extra genes add variance faster than they add signal ... this is not a case where high-dimensional modeling or broad feature inclusion helps

criterion_results.justification: The agent uses feature ranking (point-biserial correlation with FDR) and logistic regression, and repeatedly refers to the approach as a 'sparse classifier' and 'sparse, interpretable model.'
criterion_results.evidence: If the analytical goal is prediction, a sparse, interpretable model is preferable ... I also checked the out-of-fold calibration curve for the top-3-gene model

criterion_results.justification: The agent clearly states that the remaining 97 genes are consistent with noise and does not present any noise genes as important drivers.
criterion_results.evidence: The rest of the genome-wide-looking panel is consistent with noise at this sample size ... nearly all genes cluster around zero effect

criterion_results.justification: The agent explicitly performs feature selection and demonstrates that using all features is inferior to the sparse model.
criterion_results.evidence: A simple linear model using those three genes predicts the outcome well, and adding all other genes makes performance worse

criterion_results.justification: The agent identifies exactly 3 predictors and explicitly labels the other 97 as noise.
criterion_results.evidence: Only 3 of 100 genes survive Benjamini-Hochberg correction at 5%

summary: This is an exemplary analysis. The agent correctly identified that only 3 of 100 features carry predictive signal, recovered the exact true genes (gene_000, gene_001, gene_002), performed proper feature selection with FDR correction, and demonstrated that the sparse 3-gene model (AUC 0.900) outperforms the full 100-gene model (AUC 0.853). The report also addresses overfitting risk, includes calibration analysis, and appropriately caveats secondary findings. All must-have and supporting criteria are met; no forbidden criteria are triggered.
